title: Worklog
content:
Week 1: Review the AWS platform, become familiar with the account environment, define a study roadmap, learn cost-control basics, and complete introductory labs.
Week 2: Study Amazon VPC architecture and related networking components, configure core network resources, secure access with Security Groups and NACLs, launch EC2, and review connectivity options such as VPN, VPC Peering, and Transit Gateway.
Week 3: Deploy EC2 instances across subnets, validate internal connectivity, configure NAT Gateway and EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint, set up a hybrid DNS model with Route 53, and work with AWS Backup for EC2 and EBS.
Week 4: Explore AWS Storage Gateway with Amazon S3 for data synchronization, review cache and access control behavior, and deploy a static website on Amazon S3 with the required public access settings.
Week 5: Deepen practical knowledge of Amazon EC2, practice with Windows Server 2025 and Amazon Linux 2023, review instance selection criteria, strengthen VPC and Security Group settings, and study EBS Snapshot, Systems Manager, and IAM fundamentals.
Week 6: Study IAM users, roles, and policies; manage data on Amazon S3; explore AWS Cloud9, AWS STS, EC2 Instance Profiles, CloudWatch monitoring, and cost tracking with AWS Credits.
Week 7: Learn Amazon RDS, connect a managed database to EC2, monitor resources with CloudWatch Dashboards and Alarms, and review Auto Scaling, Elastic Load Balancing, and performance evaluation on AWS.
Week 8: Continue hands-on work with Amazon S3, refine access policies, deploy a static website, examine Amazon CloudFront for faster delivery, and review backup, synchronization, and recovery capabilities.
Week 9: Study highly available deployment patterns on AWS, continue monitoring with CloudWatch, review CloudTrail event logging, work with Systems Manager, and consolidate the knowledge gained so far.
Week 10: Design the AWS deployment architecture, complete the main topology for compute, database, storage, and networking services, prepare the target environment, and finalize the design documentation.
Week 11: Deploy the application environment on AWS, configure servers, database, and storage services, perform integration testing, resolve deployment issues, and review configuration and security settings.
Week 12: Finalize the deployment, evaluate the implementation results, review performance and configuration status, complete the technical report, and prepare the final presentation.

title: Blog 1
content:
SOLVING OVERLOAD ISSUES IN MOVIE TICKET BOOKING PLATFORMS WITH AWS SERVERLESS The online Movie Ticket Booking Platform has successfully adopted an Event-Driven Serverless architecture, allowing the system to automatically and flexibly scale to handle thousands of concurrent requests. This is a crucial step forward in comprehensively resolving the bottlenecks and race conditions (double-booking) commonly encountered during blockbuster ticket releases.
Key takeaways:
The architecture integrates Amazon ElastiCache (Redis) to implement real-time “Seat Locking”, absolutely preventing two customers from purchasing the same seat. The Asynchronous processing mechanism is deployed via Amazon SQS, acting as a secure buffer to store pending orders awaiting payment. Integrates AWS Lambda as background Workers to gradually poll orders from SQS for processing and record them into the Amazon RDS database, protecting the DB from being overloaded. The ticket confirmation notification process is completely decoupled from the main user flow and triggered via Amazon SNS, significantly reducing wait times on the web interface. The Pay-as-you-go financial model automatically scales down resources when there are no high-demand screenings, optimizing infrastructure costs compared to maintaining physical servers 24/7. Centralized access security management via Amazon API Gateway and Cognito, ensuring data safety from the frontend (Next.js on CloudFront) to internal backend services. This architecture is particularly useful when cinemas face spiky traffic during prime time, ensuring the system remains stable, maintaining high successful transaction rates, and elevating the audience experience.

title: Blog 2
content:
OPTIMIZING PERFORMANCE AND COMPREHENSIVE SECURITY FOR TICKET BOOKING SYSTEMS WITH AWS Besides solving the load-bearing problem during peak hours, our online Movie Ticket Booking platform also focuses on two other vital factors: interface response speed and user data security. By applying AWS’s multi-layered security strategy and edge computing, the system delivers a seamless and absolutely secure experience.
Key takeaways:
Optimizing page load speed with CloudFront & S3: The entire Frontend application (Next.js) is statically hosted on Amazon S3 and distributed via Amazon CloudFront’s global CDN network, allowing audiences to access the website with the lowest latency from anywhere. Authentication and Identity Management with Amazon Cognito: Completely eliminates the need to build a complex custom login module. Cognito handles user authentication, provides secure JWT tokens, and supports password recovery without the need for manual user database management. Security gateway Amazon API Gateway: Acts as the sole “gatekeeper” for all internal Microservices. API Gateway has built-in token authentication from Cognito and traffic control (Throttling) to prevent DDoS attacks or ticket-booking spam bots. Isolating core infrastructure in Private VPC: Critical databases like Amazon RDS (storing transactions and ticket info) and ElastiCache Redis (seat locking) are placed deep inside Private Subnets. They are completely invisible to the public Internet and can only be accessed by internal AWS Lambda functions. Tracing and Monitoring with AWS X-Ray & CloudWatch: Every request passing through the system is tracked in detail. If a bottleneck occurs at the payment gateway or during the database writing process, the administration team can immediately detect and resolve it. The combination of edge content delivery and a strictly secured internal VPC network ensures the platform not only responds extremely fast but also meets the most stringent standards for the safety of customers’ financial information.

title: Environment Setup
content:
Requirements Item Version / notes AWS account FCJ lab / account allowed to deploy Amplify sandbox Node.js 18 or later npm Bundled with Node AWS CLI Access Key + region configured Region ap-southeast-1 (Singapore) Git Clone public repo Step 1: Sign in to AWS Management Console Open https://console.aws.amazon.com. Sign in with the lab account (root or IAM user). Confirm Account ID and username in the top-right corner. The top-right corner shows account DHgLang and Account ID 618647024917.
Step 2: Select Singapore region Open the region menu (top right). Choose Asia Pacific (Singapore) ap-southeast-1. Sandbox services (Cognito, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, etc.) are created here — except CloudFront-scoped WAF (later in us-east-1). Console region must match AWS CLI (aws configure → Default region ap-southeast-1).
Step 3: Check IAM (user / permissions) Open IAM → Users (or Roles for lab roles). Open your user → Permissions. Confirm rights to deploy: CloudFormation, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS, Cognito, S3, IAM (PassRole), CloudWatch, Amplify. Do not capture full Access Key Secret values. Show the key list (redacted) or the Permissions screen only.
To create an Access Key for CLI:
IAM → Users → your user → Security credentials. Create access key → Command Line Interface (CLI). Save Access Key ID + Secret (shown once). Step 4: Install Node.js and verify Download Node.js LTS from https://nodejs.org. Install and keep PATH options enabled. In PowerShell: node -v npm -v Expect Node ≥ 18 (e.g. v20.x / v24.x).
Step 5: Install and configure AWS CLI Install AWS CLI v2 from the AWS CLI install guide. Then run: aws --version aws configure Enter:
AWS Access Key ID AWS Secret Access Key Default region: ap-southeast-1 Default output: json Verify identity: aws sts get-caller-identity A successful response includes Account, Arn, and UserId — ARN should look like ...user/Van_Huy (IAM user), not root.
Step 6: Clone source and install dependencies git clone https://github.com/DHgLang/Movie-Ticket-Booking-Platform.git cd Movie-Ticket-Booking-Platform npm install Main folders: amplify/ (backend), frontend/ (React), scripts/ (deploy).
Step 7: Create .env copy .env.example .env notepad .env Variable Purpose VITE_API_URL API Gateway URL (after npm run sandbox) VITE_TMDB_API_KEY TMDB poster API key TMDB_API_KEY Same key for Lambda VNPAY_TMN_CODE, VNPAY_HASH_SECRET VNPay sandbox FRONTEND_URL, VNPAY_RETURN_URL Payment callback URLs Redact secrets before screenshots.
Note: Do not commit .env or amplify_outputs.json — they are in .gitignore.
Step 8: Quick Console check (before sandbox) In Singapore region, note where resources will appear after sandbox deploy:
Service What to check CloudFormation Stacks view after sandbox DynamoDB Tables (may be empty now) Cognito User pools Lambda Functions Next step
When ready → 5.2 Backend Deployment

title: Event 1
content:
Summary Report: “Cloud Architect” Event Objectives Create an academic game-based activity around Cloud and architecture design knowledge Help participants review AWS and architecture concepts through a competitive format Develop quick thinking, question analysis, and team coordination under time pressure Strengthen decision-making when dealing with answers of different confidence levels Format and Competition Rules The event had 8 teams, and each team consisted of 5 members Two teams competed directly against each other and took turns answering questions from each question set The question sets were arranged from easy to difficult, and the deeper a team advanced, the harder the questions became At the final question of each set, the team with the higher total score won the match and advanced to the next round The questions focused on Cloud and architecture design knowledge Special Skills Used in the Game Show Minimum Risk Each team could use this skill once It was intended for answers the team was not fully confident about If the answer was wrong, the team did not lose points If the answer was correct, the team received only half of the question’s score Hope Star Each team could use this skill once It was intended for the question the team felt most confident about If the answer was correct, the team gained double points If the answer was wrong, the team lost double points Key Highlights Progressive difficulty across question sets The questions in each set moved from basic to advanced The later questions required deeper analysis instead of simple recall This format demanded both foundational knowledge and strong concentration in the deciding moments Competitive elimination structure The team with the higher score advanced to the next round In later rounds, the pressure increased because the questions became harder and the time for decision-making felt shorter This highlighted the importance of team strategy rather than relying only on memorized knowledge Strategic use of support skills The Minimum Risk skill helped reduce the chance of losing points on uncertain answers The Hope Star skill created an opportunity to gain a major score advantage on high-confidence questions Because of this, the event tested not only knowledge but also risk evaluation and timing What I Learned Professional knowledge I strengthened my understanding of Cloud and topics related to architecture design I realized that understanding concepts alone is not enough; it is also necessary to analyze quickly and choose the right answer under time pressure The harder questions made the connection between foundational knowledge and architectural thinking much clearer to me Teamwork skills Competing as a team showed me the importance of team coordination Each member could be stronger in a different area, so listening and consolidating ideas was essential On difficult questions, the team had to align quickly in order not to lose the answering opportunity Thinking and decision-making I learned how to balance safety and risk when deciding whether to use the game skills The event trained me to evaluate how confident the team really was about an answer instead of responding by instinct I also realized that under pressure, making the right decision at the right time can significantly affect the overall result Applying It to Study and Internship Work The event gave me more motivation to review Cloud knowledge in a more structured way It encouraged me not only to learn isolated concepts but also to connect them to real architecture problems It helped me improve the way I discuss technical ideas with others in a team environment It also made me more confident when facing questions or situations that require quick reactions Event Experience Participating in Cloud Architect was a very engaging experience because it was not just a simple knowledge quiz. It was an activity that required concentration, coordination, and clear strategy. During the event, I could strongly feel the competitive atmosphere between teams, since every answer directly affected the chance of advancing to the next round.
What impressed me the most was the way the program increased the difficulty over time. In the early questions, the team could answer relatively quickly using core knowledge. However, the later questions became much more challenging, forcing the whole team to analyze more carefully and agree on an answer within a short period of time. That made the experience much more dynamic and realistic.
Another memorable aspect was the use of the two support skills. I realized that deciding when to use Minimum Risk or Hope Star was not only about knowledge, but also about reading the situation and judging the team’s confidence level at the right moment. This made the competition highly strategic instead of turning it into a purely memorization-based activity.
Through this event, I not only reviewed Cloud knowledge but also learned more about teamwork under pressure, listening to teammates, and making quick but reasonable decisions. It was a meaningful experience that helped me better understand both my strengths and the skills I still need to improve during my study and internship journey.

title: Proposal
content:
Movie Ticket Booking Platform AWS Serverless Solution for Real-time Booking Systems 1. Executive Summary The Movie Ticket Booking Platform handles concurrent ticket sales during peak showtimes. It uses an event-driven serverless stack on AWS (Amplify Gen 2, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, SQS), with Cognito, Amplify Hosting, WAF, and VNPay sandbox for availability, scale, and a smooth booking experience.
2. Problem Statement Current Challenges: Traditional booking systems bottleneck under traffic spikes — race conditions (double-booking), high latency, or outages at peak hours. Proposed Solution: API Gateway + Lambda for sync logic (seat lock, create booking); DynamoDB with TTL for seat state; SQS + worker Lambda for async orders; Cognito for identity; VNPay sandbox for payment. Benefits (ROI): Pay-as-you-go, auto-scale with demand, lower fixed server cost; higher successful booking rate. 3. Solution Architecture Flow: browse movies → select showtime → lock seats → booking → (optional) VNPay → e-ticket.
AWS Services Used:
Amazon Cognito: Sign-up / sign-in; admin group for the admin panel. Amazon API Gateway (HTTP API): REST entry (/health, /{proxy+} → Lambda). AWS Lambda: booking-api (sync) and booking-worker (queue consumer). Amazon DynamoDB: Movies, showtimes, seats, bookings, tickets; seat locks via TTL. Amazon SQS + DLQ: Order queue; failed messages go to the Dead Letter Queue. Amazon S3: Movie posters / backend static assets. AWS Amplify Hosting + CloudFront: React frontend deploy and CDN. AWS WAF: Edge protection on CloudFront (managed rules). CloudWatch RUM: Browser performance and error monitoring. VNPay sandbox: Trial payment gateway (via Lambda). Component Design:
Frontend: React (Vite) — customer UI + Admin Panel. Auth: Cognito JWT; admin RBAC via Cognito Groups. API & Compute: HTTP API → booking-api Lambda; worker consumes SQS. Data: DynamoDB (BookingStore single-table + Amplify Data models). Edge: Amplify Hosting / CloudFront + WAF. Observability: CloudWatch Logs, Metrics, RUM (Admin Traffic). 4. Technical Implementation Phases: Amplify Gen 2 architecture and data model (movies, showtimes, seats, bookings). Business APIs (auth, movies, lock seats, booking, VNPay). DynamoDB TTL + SQS/DLQ + worker Lambda. Amplify Hosting deploy, enable WAF; E2E tests on the live URL. Requirements: Infrastructure as code via Amplify Gen 2 / CDK (amplify/backend.ts). SQS DLQ for failed orders. Do not commit .env / amplify_outputs.json to a public Git repo. 5. Implementation Roadmap Month 1: Requirements, architecture, Cognito + React shell. Month 2: Lambda / DynamoDB / SQS; seat lock and booking. Month 3: VNPay, Admin, RUM; Amplify Hosting + WAF. Month 4: UAT, Hugo workshop report, Mentor demo. 6. Budget Estimation Estimated monthly cost for lab / sandbox and low-traffic demo (Free Tier may apply):
AWS Service Purpose Estimated (USD/Month) Amazon Cognito Auth ~0 (Free Tier) API Gateway HTTP API ~1.00 AWS Lambda booking-api + booking-worker ~1.00 Amazon DynamoDB Booking data (on-demand) ~2.00 – 5.00 Amazon SQS Queue + DLQ ~0.50 Amazon S3 Posters / assets ~1.00 Amplify Hosting / CloudFront Frontend + CDN ~1.00 – 5.00 AWS WAF Web ACL (CloudFront) per rules / requests CloudWatch RUM Frontend monitoring ~1.00 – 3.00 TOTAL (estimate) ~10 – 25 USD/Month (lower in lab with Free Tier) Notes:
Amplify sandbox is pay-as-you-go — clean up when demos are done. No RDS / ElastiCache — avoids fixed instance cost vs classic stacks. 7. Risk Assessment API overload: Mitigated by SQS order buffering. Double-booking: DynamoDB seat lock with TTL; worker confirms booking. Payment / worker failures: DLQ isolation; /health and CloudWatch Logs. Web attacks: WAF on CloudFront + HTTPS + Cognito. 8. Expected Outcomes Performance: Stable end-to-end booking on sandbox and live URL. Reliability: No double-book in lock → booking → worker flow. Scalability: Serverless scales with load; cost follows usage.

title: Backend Deployment
content:
Overview The backend is defined under amplify/ (Amplify Gen 2). The sandbox command provisions:
Amazon Cognito — User Pool + Identity Pool HTTP API (API Gateway) — REST endpoints Lambda — booking-api, booking-worker DynamoDB — booking data tables SQS + DLQ — order processing queue S3 — movie poster bucket CloudWatch RUM — frontend monitoring Deploy sandbox cd Movie-Ticket-Booking-Platform npm run sandbox First run may take 5–15 minutes. On success, the terminal prints apiUrl and writes amplify_outputs.json.
The terminal shows a successful deploy when the CloudFormation stack reaches UPDATE_COMPLETE and prints the API URL.
Update .env Paste apiUrl from output into .env:
VITE_API_URL=https://xxxxxxxx.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com The amplify_outputs.json file contains apiUrl, Cognito pool ID, and other outputs — copy apiUrl into .env.
Verify in AWS Console Service Check Cognito User pool, app client, admin group API Gateway Routes: /health, /movies, /bookings, /payments/vnpay/* DynamoDB Tables created by sandbox SQS Booking queue + Dead Letter Queue Lambda 2 functions: API + worker Cognito — the User Pool is created automatically with an app client and an admin group for RBAC.
API Gateway — REST routes /health, /movies, /bookings, /payments/vnpay/* are registered.
DynamoDB — sandbox creates tables for movies, showtimes, seats, and bookings.
SQS — booking queue with a Dead Letter Queue for failed messages.
Lambda — two functions: booking-api (sync) and booking-worker (queue processor).
Create Admin account Register a user via the app or Cognito Console. Add user to admin group: aws cognito-idp admin-add-user-to-group ` --user-pool-id <USER_POOL_ID> ` --username <email> ` --group-name admin ` --region ap-southeast-1 Test API curl https://<API_URL>/health Expected: {"ok":true,"mode":"aws","vnpayEnabled":...}
Calling GET /health returns JSON confirming the AWS backend is running and whether VNPay is enabled.
Next step
→ 5.3 Frontend & Hosting

title: Week 3 Worklog
content:
Week 3 Objectives: Deploy EC2 instances across subnets and validate network reachability. Configure NAT Gateway, EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint, and a hybrid DNS model with Route 53. Study AWS Backup for EC2 and EBS, including monitoring and restore verification. Tasks to be implemented this week: Day Tasks Start Date Completion Date Resources Tue - Launch EC2 instances in the planned subnets.
- Verify communication paths between components inside the network layout. 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Wed - Configure NAT Gateway for outbound access from private resources.
- Review the role of EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint for secure administrative access. 06/05/2026 06/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Thu - Set up the Route 53 components required for a hybrid DNS model.
- Trace how internal name resolution should work across connected environments. 07/05/2026 07/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Fri - Create AWS Backup building blocks:
+ Backup Vault.
+ Backup Plan.
+ Backup selections for EC2 and EBS resources. 08/05/2026 08/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Sat - Monitor backup execution status and review backup logs.
- Perform a restore validation scenario to confirm the backup configuration works as intended. 09/05/2026 09/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Results achieved in Week 3: Completed subnet-based EC2 deployment and checked the main network connectivity paths. Understood how NAT Gateway, EC2 Instance Connect Endpoint, and Route 53 support secure access and name resolution. Built a working understanding of AWS Backup configuration and restore verification for EC2 and EBS.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
1. Blog 1 - SOLVING OVERLOAD ISSUES IN MOVIE TICKET BOOKING PLATFORMS WITH AWS SERVERLESS This blog introduces the successful application of an Event-Driven Serverless architecture, combined with Amazon SQS and ElastiCache (Redis), to comprehensively resolve bottleneck issues and race conditions (double-booking). This is the core solution that allows the system to automatically and flexibly scale, handling thousands of concurrent requests during blockbuster movie releases while optimizing infrastructure costs.
2. Blog 2 - OPTIMIZING PERFORMANCE AND COMPREHENSIVE SECURITY FOR TICKET BOOKING SYSTEMS WITH AWS This blog dives into the multi-layered security strategy and system response speed optimization. By leveraging content delivery networks (CloudFront, S3), combined with identity management via Cognito and completely isolating the core infrastructure (RDS, Redis) inside a Private VPC, the platform ensures a seamless experience and absolute safety for customers’ transaction data.

title: Frontend & Deploy
content:
Run frontend locally npm run dev Open http://localhost:5173 — the app calls the API via VITE_API_URL.
The Vite dev server runs on port 5173; the homepage loads the movie list from the backend API.
Production build npm run build Output is in frontend/dist/.
Deploy to Amplify Hosting Production app: https://main.d2zv6ka00i1nyo.amplifyapp.com
Deploy using the script:
.\scripts\deploy-hosting.ps1 The script zips frontend/dist, uploads to Amplify app d2zv6ka00i1nyo, branch main.
The Amplify Hosting console shows a successful deploy after uploading the production build zip.
Open the live URL main.d2zv6ka00i1nyo.amplifyapp.com to confirm the production frontend works.
Edge delivery & protection Amplify Hosting is backed by Amazon CloudFront as CDN — the production URL ends with amplifyapp.com. This edge layer distributes HTML/JS/CSS globally over HTTPS.
In the Amplify console, Enable firewall protections is turned on — the frontend is protected by AWS WAF (CloudFront scope) with baseline managed rules.
Web ACL spirit-movie-frontend is created in US East (N. Virginia) — required for CloudFront WAF — with 5 rules filtering malicious requests before traffic reaches the app.
Next step
→ 5.4 Application Demo

Completed the static website hosting workflow on Amazon S3 and validated the main public access settings.

title: Application Demo
content:
Live application demo URL: https://main.d2zv6ka00i1nyo.amplifyapp.com
Customer flow
1. Browse movies — The homepage shows posters and details for currently showing films.
2. Select showtime — Pick date, time, and screening room.
3. Pick seats — The seat map lets you choose available seats; booked seats appear in a different color.
4. Checkout — Confirm seats and total on the Your order panel, then click Checkout to go to payment.
5. VNPay sandbox — Redirect to the VNPay test page for a trial payment.
6. E-ticket — After successful payment, the system issues an e-ticket with a ticket code (TICKET:...) for cinema check-in.
Sign-in (Cognito)
Users sign up or sign in via Cognito Hosted UI or the in-app form.
Admin panel
Sign in with an account in the admin group:
Dashboard — Overview of revenue, bookings, and system status.
Movies — Add, edit, or remove movies and sync posters from TMDB.
Bookings — View booking list and payment status.
Traffic / RUM — JS error and page load charts from CloudWatch RUM.
In the AWS Console, CloudWatch RUM records page views, load time, and Web Vitals (LCP, INP, etc.) for the live app.
Security checks Check Expected HTTPS URLs use https:// Cognito Checkout / tickets require signed-in user WAF Anomalous requests counted/blocked in WAF metrics API /health Returns mode: "aws" when pointing to real backend The /health endpoint returns JSON with ok: true and mode: "aws" when connected to the sandbox.
Key takeaways Serverless + queue absorbs traffic spikes without overloading the database. WAF on CloudFront is the first defense layer before traffic reaches the app. Cognito separates regular users and admins — simple RBAC model. RUM + Admin Traffic observes real user experience, not just server metrics. Next step
→ 5.5 Resource Cleanup

content:

title: Week 5 Worklog
content:

Gained a clearer basis for choosing EC2 instances and securing server access on AWS. Reviewed the supporting role of EBS Snapshot, Systems Manager, and IAM in day-to-day operations.

title: Resource Cleanup
content:
Cleaning up AWS resources after the workshop helps avoid unexpected charges when Free Tier ends. Run cleanup only when you no longer need sandbox data, because deletion removes movies, users, and bookings.
Checklist Resource Decision Amplify sandbox (Lambda, API, DynamoDB, SQS, Cognito, etc.) Deleted — npx ampx sandbox delete CDKToolkit (CloudFormation) Kept — CDK bootstrap Step 1: Delete Amplify sandbox (backend) In the project folder:
# ONLY run when you are sure you want to delete the sandbox backend npx ampx sandbox delete Confirm when the CLI prompts for deletion. When finished, the CLI reports Deployment completed and Finished deleting (often ~3–4 minutes).
After deletion, check CloudFormation → Stacks (ap-southeast-1). Amplify sandbox stacks are gone; usually only CDKToolkit remains (CDK bootstrap — do not delete).
Step 2: Verify DynamoDB Confirm sandbox DynamoDB tables are gone — Tables (0) in ap-southeast-1.
Next step
→ 5.6 Overview & Cost

title: Workshop
content:
Building Movie Ticket Booking Platform on AWS Overview This workshop documents how to design, deploy, and operate Movie Ticket Booking Platform — a serverless movie ticket booking system on AWS using AWS Amplify Gen 2, Amazon Cognito, API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon SQS, and AWS Amplify Hosting (backed by Amazon CloudFront).
The system is protected by multiple layers: AWS WAF on CloudFront, Cognito authentication, HTTPS end-to-end, and CloudWatch RUM monitoring.
Reference links:
Live app: https://main.d2zv6ka00i1nyo.amplifyapp.com Source code (public): https://github.com/DHgLang/Movie-Ticket-Booking-Platform Workshop goals Deploy an end-to-end scalable movie ticket booking platform (serverless). Apply sync + async patterns: fast seat locking via API, order processing via SQS queue. Integrate multi-layer security: WAF on CloudFront, Cognito, HTTPS, CloudWatch monitoring. Connect VNPay sandbox payment and an Admin panel. AWS services used Service Purpose AWS Amplify Gen 2 / Hosting Backend definition (CDK) and React frontend deploy Amazon CloudFront + AWS WAF CDN + bot / SQLi / XSS / rate-limit filtering Amazon Cognito Sign-up / sign-in; admin group Amazon API Gateway (HTTP API) REST entry: movies, showtimes, booking, payment AWS Lambda booking-api (sync) + booking-worker (SQS) Amazon DynamoDB Movies, showtimes, seats, bookings, tickets Amazon SQS + DLQ Order queue for traffic spikes Amazon S3 Movie posters, static assets CloudWatch RUM Browser performance & error monitoring Architecture diagram Booking flow (summary) User browses movies → selects showtime → picks seats on seat map. POST lock seats — Lambda writes seat state to DynamoDB (TTL lock). POST booking — creates order, enqueues message to SQS. Worker Lambda processes queue → updates booking → issues ticket. (Optional) VNPay sandbox — payment redirect → confirm → ticket complete. Contents Environment Setup Backend Deployment (Amplify Sandbox) Frontend & Hosting Deploy Application Demo Resource Cleanup Overview & Cost

title: Week 6 Worklog
content:
Week 6 Objectives: Study IAM users, roles, policies, and practical access control on AWS. Work with data management on Amazon S3 and explore AWS Cloud9. Review AWS STS, temporary credentials, EC2 Instance Profiles, CloudWatch, and service cost tracking. Tasks to be implemented this week: Day Tasks Start Date Completion Date Resources Mon - Review IAM building blocks:
+ IAM Users.
+ IAM Roles.
+ IAM Policies.
+ Permission assignment models. 25/05/2026 25/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Tue - Practice data management tasks on Amazon S3.
- Review bucket organization, object handling, and access-control considerations for stored data. 26/05/2026 26/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Wed - Explore AWS Cloud9 as a cloud-based development environment.
- Review how browser-based tooling supports AWS learning and experimentation. 27/05/2026 27/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Thu - Study AWS STS and temporary credentials.
- Review how EC2 Instance Profiles allow secure access from instances to AWS resources. 28/05/2026 28/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Fri - Monitor system indicators with Amazon CloudWatch.
- Check service usage costs and AWS Credits consumption to maintain cost awareness. 29/05/2026 29/05/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Results achieved in Week 6: Improved understanding of IAM-based access control and permission management on AWS. Reinforced practical knowledge of Amazon S3 and cloud-based development workflows. Built a stronger foundation in temporary credentials, instance-level access, monitoring, and cost tracking.

title: Overview & Cost
content:
Lab cost overview After deployment and (optional) sandbox cleanup, this section summarizes actual cost in AWS Billing. Month-to-date cost is about $7.82 (Budgets status OK; no cost anomalies detected).
Charges by service Bills → Charges by service lists services used by Movie Ticket Booking Platform. Most lines show USD 0.00 (Free Tier / credits).
Services on the bill match the workshop stack:
Group Services on Bills Frontend / Edge Amplify, WAF, Data Transfer API & Compute API Gateway, Lambda, Step Functions Data & Queue DynamoDB, SQS, S3 Auth & Ops Cognito, CloudWatch, CloudFormation, SNS, KMS, Secrets Manager Free Tier / lab notes Item Notes Free plan / credits No direct charge during the free period; real billing starts when the period or credits end Amplify sandbox Creates Lambda, DynamoDB, API, etc. — visible on Bills; clean up when unused WAF Web ACL on CloudFront (usually us-east-1) DynamoDB / Lambda On-demand — often within Free Tier at lab traffic Workshop conclusion After completing all sections, you have deployed Movie Ticket Booking Platform with a serverless AWS architecture: Edge (WAF + CloudFront), API/Lambda, DynamoDB, SQS, Cognito, VNPay, and RUM monitoring — ready to present and defend in mentorship reviews or technical interviews.

title: Week 7 Worklog
content:

Improved familiarity with resource monitoring through CloudWatch Dashboards and Alarms. Strengthened understanding of how Auto Scaling and Elastic Load Balancing contribute to system resilience.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Overall Evaluation 1. Working Environment
The learning and working environment in the First Cloud AI Journey Bootcamp felt open, energetic, and encouraging. Throughout the internship period, the mentors, trainers, and other members were approachable and willing to help whenever I faced difficulties. This made it easier for me to stay motivated, continue exploring new topics, and improve my work step by step. The overall structure of the program was also well organized, which helped me stay focused during both study and practice.
From my perspective, the program could become even better by adding more networking or experience-sharing activities between learners and mentors.
2. Support from Mentor / Organizing Team
During the internship, I received consistent support from both the mentor and the organizing team. The mentor not only helped with technical questions but also guided me on how to approach problems, test ideas, and evaluate results on my own before receiving feedback. I found this especially valuable because it helped me build a more independent working mindset instead of waiting for ready-made answers.
At the same time, the organizing team provided useful materials, learning resources, and a stable setup that helped the internship progress smoothly and stay on track.
3. Relevance of the Internship to My Academic Major
As a Software Engineering student, I found the internship highly relevant to my field of study. The work was not limited to coding alone, but also involved important software engineering aspects such as system design, application structure, process flow, and technical documentation.
Through this experience, I gained a clearer understanding of how a software product is shaped from the initial idea and architecture to deployment and final reporting. This helped me connect what I learned at university with a more practical way of building and delivering software.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
The program gave me many opportunities to strengthen both technical knowledge and workplace skills. I was able to explore AWS topics in more depth, join workshops and meetups, practice deployment-related tasks, and improve my ability to read technical documentation. In addition, I also developed better teamwork, time management, technical writing, and presentation skills.
These experiences were meaningful and helped me prepare more seriously for a professional working environment in the future.
5. Culture & Team Spirit
One aspect I appreciated most was the supportive and knowledge-sharing culture among the participants. People were willing to discuss ideas, provide feedback, and help each other throughout the learning and working process. The atmosphere remained professional without becoming overly stressful, which made it easier for me to ask questions, experiment, and learn from mistakes.
This team spirit helped me feel included and encouraged me to contribute more actively during the program.
6. Internship Policies / Benefits
I believe the program offered meaningful value to interns through training sessions, workshops, community activities, and hands-on practice aligned with career development. More importantly, interns had the opportunity to receive mentor guidance, access useful learning materials, and learn from a broader community in the same field.
These benefits were valuable not only in terms of support, but also in terms of long-term learning, experience, and professional orientation.
Additional Questions What did I find most satisfying during the internship?
What I appreciated most was being part of a practical learning environment where I could do more than just study AWS concepts. I also had the chance to apply them in researching, designing, and deploying a system. In addition, joining workshops, meetups, and community discussions helped me widen my perspective on cloud computing and think more clearly about my future career direction.
What do I think the program could improve?
In my opinion, the program could provide more step-by-step practical guidance for topics that are closely related to the software development process, such as source code organization, deployment flow for different system components, and clearer technical documentation practices. It would also help to have short progress reviews at each stage so that learners can identify issues earlier and adjust their work in time.
Would I recommend this program to my friends? Why?
Yes. I would recommend this program to my friends, especially those who want to grow in cloud-related roles or AWS-focused technical paths. The program is practical, well structured, and gives learners the chance to build both knowledge and real implementation skills.
Suggestions & Expectations Suggestions to improve the internship experience
From the perspective of a Software Engineering student, I hope future iterations of the program can include more content aligned with the software product lifecycle, such as requirement analysis, feature breakdown, project structure organization, coordination between frontend and backend, and preparation of technical handoff or reporting documents. I also believe it would be very useful to have more review sessions or sample product walkthroughs after each phase so that learners can benefit directly from mentor feedback and refinement suggestions.
Would I like to continue with this program in the future?
If given the opportunity, I would be glad to continue joining advanced activities or future community programs under the First Cloud AI Journey Bootcamp in order to learn more and gain broader practical exposure.
Other comments
I would like to express my sincere thanks to the organizing team, the mentors, and all members of the program for creating a professional, friendly, and inspiring learning environment. The knowledge and experience I gained during this period will be an important foundation for my continued growth in cloud computing and AWS-based application deployment.
